title: Counting sort

content.problemStatement:
You are given a list of integers. Your task is to sort the list based on the frequency of each integer in ascending order. If two integers have the same frequency, the one with the smaller value should come first.


Input Format

The first line contains a single integer nnn, the number of integers in the list.
The second line contains nnn space separated integers a1,a2,…,ana1,a2,…,ana1,a2,…,an.


Output Format
Print the sorted list based on the frequency of each integer.
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains an integer n (1 ≤ n ≤ 100), the number of integers in the list. The second line contains n space‑separated integers a1, a2, …, an (1 ≤ ai ≤ 100).
content.outputFormat: For each test case print the integers of the list sorted by increasing frequency. If two integers have the same frequency, the smaller integer comes first. Numbers of a test case are printed on one line separated by a single space.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Frequencies: 1→1, 2→2, 3→1. Sorted by frequency gives 1 and 3 first (both freq 1) and 2 later (freq 2). Between 1 and 3 the smaller value 1 comes first.
Frequencies: 1→1, 2→2, 3→2. 1 comes first, then 2 and 3 are ordered by value because they share the same frequency.

solution.approach: Count the occurrences of each value (the range is only 1‑100, so a simple array works). Build a list of (value,frequency) pairs for the values that appear. Sort this list with a comparator that first compares frequency (ascending) and then value (ascending). Finally, output each value repeated its frequency times in the order given by the sorted list. The algorithm is linear in the size of the input for counting and O(k log k) for sorting, where k ≤ 100 is the number of distinct values.
solution.timeComplexity: O(n + k log k) per test case, where n is the length of the list and k ≤ 100 is the number of distinct values. Since k is bounded by 100, this is effectively O(n).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(k) additional space for the frequency array and the list of pairs (k ≤ 100).

code.Java: import java.io.*;import java.util.*;public class Main{public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception{BufferedReader br=new BufferedReader(new InputStreamReader(System.in));StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder();int T=Integer.parseInt(br.readLine().trim());while(T-- >0){int n=Integer.parseInt(br.readLine().trim());StringTokenizer st=new StringTokenizer(br.readLine());int[] freq=new int[101];for(int i=0;i<n;i++){int x=Integer.parseInt(st.nextToken());freq[x]++;}List<int[]> list=new ArrayList<>();for(int v=1;v<=100;v++)if(freq[v]>0)list.add(new int[]{v,freq[v]});list.sort((a,b)->{if(a[1]!=b[1])return Integer.compare(a[1],b[1]);return Integer.compare(a[0],b[0]);});boolean first=true;for(int[] p:list){for(int i=0;i<p[1];i++){if(!first)sb.append(' ');sb.append(p[0]);first=false;}}sb.append('\n');}System.out.print(sb.toString());}}
